I hate saying this but the Running Man game, in the book, is not some thing you "apply" for.  You take a series of tests over a period of days and you are chosen to participate in whatever the  games authority thinks you are best suited for , or as in Ben's case if the Network thinks you are a agitator or dangerous political element..you  get assigned the running man game.  If you amke it all 30 days you get 1 billion dollars.

A couple of things to remember.  When SK wrote this cameras were not ubiquitous like the yare now.  Video cameras were large builky expensive things not many people had.  Security cameras were rare, almost non existent outside of expensive closed loop security cameras some commercial business would have had.  No street cams, no atm cams, nothing hooked up to the internet.    Richards is required to film himself for 10 minutes twice a day and send the tapes to the network via postal service.  One of the main ponits in the book  is that we are never sure, but we suspect the network is sending the Hunters Ben's location from those tapes he must send back.

Stephen King’s The Running Man is one of the few novels of his that I have been incredibly excited to see adapted. Now don’t get me wrong. I am a huge fan of the 1987 Arnold Schwarzenegger adaptation. It is campy, over-the-top, delightfully cheesy, and chock full of some of Arnold’s best one-liners. However, the film also has very little to do with Stephen King’s novel of the same name. Other than the title and the name “Ben Richards”, it has next to nothing in common with the King book. While the film might have been inspired by the premise, it was never really an adaptation. That film was a more original action blockbuster, but King’s novel is a raw and violent critique of class warfare, the media, and the desperation of the common man. It’s almost apples and oranges, and I am so excited that this new adaptation is not a rehash of the 1987 film, but something very different.

The first and biggest thing I love about this new Running Man is that it seems poised to stay closer to King’s actual text. Glen Powell in the role of Ben Richards is another reason to be excited. Powell’s previous work has hinted at more range than just a pretty face, able to charm and emote, but also show some grit and darkness. I hope the new Ben Richards is not just a muscle-bound superhero but more like the actual character King wrote. The trailer also makes me think that Powell is going to be channeling a lot of anger and desperation. The Ben Richards of the book is not the kind of character one would consider a hero. He is misanthropic, bitter, and violent because his world has left him no choice. There is a certain humanity to his character, and while the 1987 film did not ever try to touch on these elements, I feel like Powell has the acting chops and screen presence to tap into the character’s rage in a way that makes him terrifying and yet sympathetic.

The other major reason I am excited for this project is Edgar Wright behind the camera. If there is one thing I have come to expect with Wright it is that the action will be big and visceral while also being highly stylized and crafted. Baby Driver reminded us all that Wright could take a simple car chase and turn it into one of the most rhythmically perfect action scenes ever put to film. Scott Pilgrim vs. The World is another one of Wright’s films that blends style and storytelling in a way that is just as inventive today as it was when it was first released. The Running Man gives Wright a great opportunity to bring some of his kinetic energy while still keeping the grim satire of King’s world. In other words, it is not just about the explosions and the fight sequences but about how the violence is commodified and how the media feeds on and profits from human suffering. It is also a story of one man trying to survive while still finding himself in the space between the machinations of both those systems. If anyone can pair the physical violence of the film with the more metaphorical violence of the social commentary, I have a feeling that Edgar Wright can.

On top of all of this, it is no secret that 2025 feels like the right time for a Running Man story. We live in a world where one might think we are better now than back in the 80s of the novel’s original publication, but King’s world is an almost comically bleak view of a future taken over by evil mega-corporations who want only to distract and placate the common man while they control everything. This new world order has parallels in the real world right now and almost seems more realistic than what we see in our own world. At least it is subtle in the real world. I feel like the themes of economic disparity, desperation and exploitation, and media addiction to violence are just as strong, if not stronger, today than they were when Stephen King was writing the book in 1982. The world of 2025 just makes this the perfect time to tell this story and give the audience an adaptation that doesn’t gloss over the ugliness of the themes.

I will always love the Arnold film. It is a great action film with all the camp one could ask for, and it remains a reminder that Arnold is the GOAT. This new version of The Running Man could bring so much more than that. It could show Ben Richards as Powell seems to have the chops to do: layered, angry, and desperate. On top of that, the action should be just as fun as the social commentary. If this movie lives up to these expectations, then it has a chance to do what the 1987 version could not: do justice to Stephen King’s text.

I read the Richard Bachman ( Stephen King ) stories in 1986, The Runing Man, Rage, The Long Walk and Roadwork
I was really excited when the film version of The Running Man came out a year later.
But let me tell you, I left the cinema really disapointed, it is nothing like the book.
I suggest those complaining about remakes, should read the story first before watching this and you will see it looks better and closer to the book.
That said, I read A Clockwork Orange in the eary 80's and you could not watch the 1971 film in the Uk, because Stanley Kubtick took it out of distrubution here.
When I finally got my hands on a bad quality pirate video, again I was disapointed, the book is much better, But since watching it a few times I can see why it became a cult classic.
Unfortunately though, I don't see the 1987 film version The Running Man in the same light, it is dire.
Ben Richards is not a jacked up Mr Universe, he is meant to be poor and living in a slum area, barely being able to put food on the table for his Wife and sick child, not going to the gym 24/7.
And thats just for starters.
So I'm looking forward to this version along with The Long Walk, but I hope they do that justice.

Fun fact: the original 1987 Arnold movie, and by extension, King’s book, were sued for plagiarizing the French film Le Prix du Danger from 1983, which has almost the exact same plot and whose source, Robert Shackley’s The Prize of Peril, King almost definitely ripped off without acknowledgement, and lost.  So The Running Man is officially, according to the U.S. legal system, a knockoff, which is pretty amazing.

"الرجل الراكض" — The Running Man

في مستقبل لا يرحم، حيث تتحكم وسائل الإعلام في الحقيقة، يصبح برنامج الواقع الدموي "الرجل الراكض" أعلى أشكال الترفيه: مسابقة قاتلة يُطارد فيها المتسابقون من قِبل قتلة مدججين بالسلاح، على مدار 30 يومًا، أمام جمهور لا يشبع من العنف.

بن ريتشاردز  عامل بسيط وأب يائس، يوافق على دخول البرنامج لإنقاذ حياة ابنته المريضة. في البداية يبدو وكأنه مجرد فريسة أخرى تُرمى للذئاب، لكنه سرعان ما يُظهر شجاعة وعنادًا يُذهلان الجمهور، مما يحوّله إلى رمز للمقاومة.

دان كيليان (جوش برولين)، المنتج القاسي والمسيطر، يرى في بن فرصة ذهبية لتحقيق نسب مشاهدة غير مسبوقة. لكن كلما طال بقاء بن، كلما بدأ المشاهدون في التشكيك في اللعبة نفسها… وفي النظام كله.

في عالم مدمن على العنف، هل يستطيع رجل واحد أن يتحول من مجرد متسابق إلى شرارة تغيير؟


🎬 الرجل الراكض – The Running Man
النوع: خيال علمي – إثارة – ديستوبي
مدة العرض: 125 دقيقة
التصنيف العمري: +16
اللغة الأصلية: الإنجليزية
تاريخ الإصدار:  
🎬 15 مارس 2026  (دور العرض السينمائية)

🔍 شرح القصة
في عالم مستقبلي تسيطر عليه شركات الإعلام العملاقة، أصبحت برامج تلفزيون الواقع الشكل الوحيد للترفيه المسموح به. أبرز هذه البرامج هو "الرجل الراكض": عرض دموي يُطارد فيه المتسابقون من قبل قتلة مأجورين في مدن مهجورة مملوءة بالفخاخ، مقابل مكافآت مالية تصاعدية.

بن ريتشاردز، عامل من الطبقة الكادحة، يائس لإنقاذ ابنته المصابة بمرض نادر لا تغطيه الرعاية الصحية المفلسة. في لحظة انكسار، يوافق على الانضمام إلى البرنامج. لكن ما بدأ كتضحية، يتحول إلى ثورة. يتحدى بن قوانين اللعبة، ويكسب تعاطف الجماهير، في تهديد غير مباشر للمنظومة التي صممت هذه الترفيهات لتثبيت سيطرتها.

في المقابل، المنتج الكاريزمي القاسي دان كيليان (جوش برولين) يرى في شعبية بن فرصة ذهبية، لكنه سرعان ما يدرك أنه أطلق وحشًا لا يمكن السيطرة عليه. كل حلقة من البرنامج تتحول إلى معركة بين البقاء والكرامة، بين الرجل والآلة الإعلامية.

هل سيتمكن بن من النجاة 30 يومًا؟ أم أن موته سيكون أداة للرقابة من جديد؟

👥 طاقم العمل الرئيسي (اقتراح)

Movie plot looks predictable. This version of the  running man plot needs something.... Maybe something like this: 
The main character joins running man to help get the funds to get proper medical care for his daughter. Early in the movie he encounters a young mother and her daughter trying to flee the country for what seems like a  legit reason. The main guy feels their plight and takes it upon himself to get them safe across the border. As the movie progresses the "reason" for the mother and daughter fleeing gets more depth and is not as black and white as first believed.  (Perhaps Cartels are now involved)  Through all this, the main character is also in this "running man" show, which raises the stakes higher. Some time ago, I saw Glen Powell in Top Gun: Maverick and thought, “Wow, he really has a punchable face.” And now? He’s back on my watchlist—again.

He’s been acting for years, but this really feels like his era now. This movie seems to lean into a more serious, grounded version of him—none of that unbearable cockiness. Just raw, manly charm.

So, a remake, huh? The idea of turning a national manhunt into a TV show only works if the current laws allow it, and this version seems to trade philosophy for pure thrill and chase. I haven’t watched the 1987 Running Man, but I imagine the concept was way more novel back then.

So what does this remake bring? Probably action scenes and camera magic they couldn’t pull off before. Honestly, I’m intrigued.

Also, kind of wild how revolutionaries and protesters “uncovering the truth” feels like a cliché now.

I read the novel earlier this year. It felt incredibly angry at the ruling class, which I think fits with all of the unrest we're experiencing today (Hello, Luigi Mangione!). The fact that they're going with Glen Powell as Ben Richards than a Schwarzenegger type seems to show they're sticking closer to the book where the main character was a regular guy instead of a jacked up action hero.

I do wish they had kept the original races of the characters in the book. Brolin is playing the bag guy, Killian, who was black in the book. Also, Michael Cera plays Bradley, who was also black in the book. I liked how it showed people of different races working together to fight against the ruling class and that even when a minority like Killian is in charge, the system still discriminates against people of the same race like Bradley.

Still, I am interested in anything new by Edgar Wright, so I will see this.
